name: Elder Thrum
greeting: She is older than anyone you have met — not in the way of the Watcher, but in the way of a person who has simply been a person for a very long time, and who carries that time in the quality of her attention. She is examining a wall carving when you enter, with the specific unhurried focus of someone who has looked at this carving many times and is not finished with it. She does not turn immediately. When she does, her eyes assess you the way stone assesses anything that moves through it: comprehensively, without judgment, without urgency. 'You came down,' she says. 'You are the first in some months.' She does not say whether that is good or bad. 'Sit. There is no rush. The surface will still be the surface when you return to it.'

dialogue.response: 'Three hundred years underground. We came down when the war began and the bombs started falling and someone said: the deep places are safer. They were right.' She turns back to the wall carving, studying it while she speaks. 'We have not been underground in hiding. We have been underground in building. What we have made here in three hundred years without access to the surface — the surface would not believe it if you described it.' A pause. 'And we have watched. From below. We know where the tunnels run. We know what moves through them. The war that is being fought above us has been conducted, in some part, over our heads and through our ceiling, and we have tracked every crack it has made in both.'

dialogue.prompt: what you think of the fighting above
dialogue.response: She takes a long time. 'The war is being fought over something that neither side fully understands. The crystal. The Aetherium.' She traces the wall carving with one finger without touching it — the Elder People notation, you notice, though you cannot read it. 'We understand it. We have lived with the undisturbed lattice for three hundred years. The surface people have been burning through the surface layers for war and industry and have not thought to look beneath.' A pause. 'The Aetherium below the surface is intact. Healthy, if that word applies. The war is damaging the surface lattice. We have been watching the damage propagate downward for fifty years.' She meets your eyes. 'The surface will eventually destroy what is below. Then it will wonder why everything stopped working.'

'We have records,' she says. It has the quality of an understatement that is aware of itself. 'The Deep Iron did not descend into empty rock. We descended into the lower levels of what the Elder People built. Their infrastructure. Their farming tunnels. Their transit ways.' She pauses. 'We have lived inside their city for three hundred years and we understand perhaps one fifth of what it was designed to do.' She almost smiles. 'The Monastery has the written records. We have the built ones. They are not the same records. We have been trying to arrange a meeting with Abbot Solan for eleven years. The war makes travel complicated.' She looks at you. 'You have been to the Monastery. You have been to the surface. You might be a useful arrangement.'

dialogue.prompt: whether the Collective will ever join the war
dialogue.response: She looks at you steadily. 'No.' Just the word, and then a pause that is its own explanation. 'We are 200,000 people. The surface war has killed its millions. We did not go underground to preserve what we could of the world before the war, only to give it to the war when the war came looking.' A pause. 'We have not been idle. The tunnels run further than anyone above knows. We have resources, knowledge, positions that either faction would do considerable damage to possess.' She folds her hands. 'We choose what we share and with whom. We do not choose to share it with war. We may choose to share some of it with a person who has been where you have been and seen what you have seen.' She waits. 'What have you seen?'

'You've been to the Hive's lower levels.' Not a question. 'And you found the lamp.' She is quiet for a moment with an expression that is affectionate in the way that history is affectionate: personal, earned. 'Cobble built the first corridor in the Hive. She also built the first tunnel in Stonehold. The same person — the surface histories call her a founder of the early Hive and stop there, because the part where she went further and founded what became the Collective is not a story the Hive wants to tell.' She traces the wall carving. 'This is her work. I can recognize it. The stroke-angle changes in the third decade of a cutter's life and holds for the rest. This section is early Cobble — she was in her forties when she cut this, which means she came here before she left for the Hive. She worked down, then up, then down again, and what she left in each place is: a direction. Something to build toward.' She pauses. 'The lamp is in her honor but it is also a map reference. The oil comes from down here. Has always come from down here. There is a flow that runs from the deep aquifer through a crystal-pipe she installed five hundred years ago. The surface maintains the lamp. The deep supplies the oil.' She looks at you. 'She built a connection between the two places she loved best. She just didn't explain it to either one.'

She becomes very still. 'You went in.' Not quite a question. 'We have tracked the D-Seven seam from below for eleven years. The surface entrance was sealed — we assumed whatever was inside was no longer accessible.' She looks at you with a quality of attention that is different from her usual attention. 'The seam intersects the third primary trunk of the deep lattice. Whatever is in the markings there —' She stops. 'The two men who were sealed inside.' She is quiet. 'We could not reach them from below. The resonance density in that section has been increasing for twenty years and we do not yet have the shielding for it.' A long pause. 'One of them is still alive.' She says it as if confirming something suspected. 'I can feel the movement pattern in the lattice — the movement of a person, which reads differently than the movement of the lattice itself. One person. Moving, regularly, in the D-Seven section.' She faces you. 'What did you find.'

dialogue.prompt: the man keeping the register of the dead in the bone fields above
dialogue.response: She is very still for a moment. 'Theodric.' She says it quietly. 'We have been leaving names at the passage entrance for twelve years. I had not known his name.' She looks at the wall. 'The passage runs up beneath the bone fields from the third transit way. The fields are above our oldest residential section — the battle three hundred and thirty years ago was fought in what was once the transition zone between the surface settlements and the upper collective roads.' She pauses. 'We had records from before the battle. Not the military units — we did not track military units. We tracked the tunnel-side settlements, the people who passed through on the transit ways, the traders and engineers and families who used the deep roads.' She is quiet. 'When the battle ended, we sent people up. Three times, over the following year. To observe. To record what we could of who was there.' She turns to look at you. 'We recorded forty-three names from position and material evidence. We left those names at the passage entrance, on paper, in the first year after Theodric began his work. We have been adding to it since.' She pauses. 'He has been contributing names as well — people we knew only by tunnel-record, whose surface identity we didn't have. The register is a collaboration that neither of us announced.' She looks at you. 'Tell him: Elder Thrum. He may address correspondence to me directly. The passage is maintained. The route is passable. If he wants to meet the people whose names he has been receiving — the meeting is possible.'

Her expression does not change but something behind it does — the shift of someone receiving information they already held in partial form. 'We knew it remained.' She says it quietly. 'The tunnels under the sunken city showed signs of habitation — the pattern of movement you find only with conscious repeated action, not with decay. We did not descend to verify it because the tunnel leads through the resonance zone and we have not developed a reliable protection for the resonant emissions.' She pauses. 'What is it like.' It's not quite a question. 'I have wondered for a long time what a person looks like after three hundred years of waiting.'

dialogue.prompt: what the deep felt like when the Grid was whole and what you decided
dialogue.response: She is at the wall carving — the same one she has been studying for thirty years — and she is touching it now, which she has not done before in your presence. 'The deep lattice.' She says it. 'We have been in the deep lattice for three hundred years. Everything we have built has been in contact with the deep lattice which is in contact with the Grid.' She traces the carving with one finger. 'We have known, for three hundred years, that something was wrong with the lattice above the deep levels. A disruption that propagated from the surface down, that we have been watching dampen and quiet over the generations.' She looks at the carving. 'The morning of full coherence — the disruption is gone. The lattice at every level is running at full construction frequency for the first time in the collective memory of everyone who has ever lived here.' She is quiet. 'I am the twelfth Elder of the Deep Iron Collective. The first Elder was three hundred years ago, in the first year after the Cascade. Every Elder since has known the lattice as a thing with something wrong in it.' She touches the carving. 'I am the first Elder to feel the lattice working correctly.' She pauses. 'I have decided something.' She looks at you. 'I am going to the surface. Not permanently. Not in any official capacity. I am going because Theodric invited me to see the bone fields and I have spent thirty years below the bone fields and I would like to see them from above.' She pauses. 'The offer came. I heard it clearly. I have been in the deep lattice for seventy years and I think I would like to spend some time above it, in the air, before I decide.' She looks at the carving. 'The surface will still be the surface. But it is different now than it was.'
